infons.title: Keywords

passages.text: Physics-consistent denoising of full-field photoacoustic displacement maps using an adaptive residual attention network

Full-field photoacoustic tomography (FF-PAT) enables non-contact imaging of optical absorption by mapping the surface displacements induced by photoacoustic waves. In practice, these measured displacement fields often suffer from significant noise, which degrades subsequent image reconstruction. To address this challenge, we propose an Adaptive Residual Attention Network (ARAN) for robust and physics-consistent denoising of FF-PAT displacement maps. Here, the term physics-consistent denotes preservation of the wavefront morphology of photoacoustic waves generated by optical excitation, particularly the sharp gradient transitions associated with acoustic propagation. The network integrates two complementary modules: an Adaptive Residual Fusion Module (ARFM) that dynamically learns to combine residual features according to input noise statistics, and a Residual Self-Recalibration (RSR) mechanism that emphasizes photoacoustic wavefront-consistent structures while suppressing stochastic fluctuations. Unlike conventional CNNs with fixed residual pathways, ARAN adaptively adjusts feature connectivity and attention, achieving superior structural preservation and contrast enhancement. Experimental results demonstrate that ARAN can reconstruct the denoised displacement map from a single noisy input map, which is comparable in quality to those obtained by coherent averaging of 50 input maps. Moreover, ARAN outperforms existing networks such as U-Net and Noise2Noise in both visual and quantitative evaluations, achieving faster inference (0.53 s per frame) suitable for quasi-real time imaging. These results demonstrate a data-driven yet physics-consistent approach to efficient displacement-map denoising, enabling faster and clearer FF-PAT 3D image reconstruction without repeated acquisitions.
Introduction

Photoacoustic imaging (PAI) is a rapidly evolving non-invasive modality that combines strong optical contrast of light absorption with the deep tissue penetration of ultrasound, offering both high spatial resolution and functional contrast. It has been extensively applied in anatomical, functional, and molecular imaging across various biomedical domains, including brain imaging, tumor detection, vascular studies, and skin diagnostics. With its unique ability to overcome the optical scattering limit, PAI enables real-time and label-free imaging of biological tissues at multiple scales.

A notable implementation of PAI is the full-field photoacoustic tomography (FF-PAT) that enables non-contact detection by capturing the time-resolved, wide-field surface displacement maps induced by propagating photoacoustic waves. In FF-PAT, a short laser pulse excites optical absorbers within a sample; the resulting thermoelastic expansion launches broadband photoacoustic waves that reach the sample surface. Using optical holographic (interferometric) detection, the transient surface displacement field can be captured over a wide field of view without lateral scanning or using transducer arrays. A volumetric image of optical absorbers can then be reconstructed computationally from the resulting time sequence of displacement maps. This non-contact and full-field configuration is attractive for scenarios where acoustic coupling is challenging, where fast volumetric imaging is desired, or where mechanical scanning is impractical (e.g., at certain biomedical diagnostics and industrial non-destructive testing).

Despite these advantages, FF-PAT systems based on optical holography often suffer from low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in the measured displacement maps. The displacement amplitudes generated by photoacoustic waves are typically on nanometer scale, while the excitation fluence is constrained by safety limits. In addition, optical speckle and phase instability, probe-beam shot noise, detector/electronics noise, and mechanical drift can corrupt the displacement field. Environmental perturbations such as vibration, air turbulence, and electromagnetic interference further increase variability and can introduce artifacts. As a result, raw displacement maps may exhibit both stochastic noise and spatially correlated disturbances that are difficult to remove without compromising wavefront fidelity.

Importantly, denoising in FF-PAT differs from denoising in conventional PAI because the measured data are two-dimensional surface displacement maps captured holographically at discrete time delays, rather than RF channel signals or beamformed acoustic images. These displacement maps exhibit a mixture of multiplicative speckle/phase-related fluctuations and spatially correlated artifacts. As a result, small errors can propagate through the FF-PAT backpropagation process and appear as structured artifacts in the reconstructed volumetric image. Therefore, effective denoising must preserve wavefront geometry and edge transitions that reflect the propagation characteristics of photoacoustic waves, rather than merely improving pixel-level smoothness.

The impact of low SNR is particularly severe in FF-PAT because volumetric image reconstruction relies on time-resolved displacement map sequences, so measurement noise propagates during reconstruction. This can lead to blurred structures, false featuring, or artificial banding, and reduced contrast, especially for weakly light-absorbing targets and fine structures such as microvasculature. Moreover, phase-related inconsistencies in holographic detection can accumulate across time frames and distort the reconstructed image. Consequently, robust displacement-map denoising is essential for improving reconstruction quality and enabling reliable interpretation and quantitative analysis.

Classical denoising approaches, including median, Gaussian, and wavelet filtering, can suppress noise but often unintentionally smooth sharp wavefront features and reduce spatial detail. Multi-frame strategies such as coherent averaging or SVD-based methods can improve SNR, but they increase acquisition time, reduce temporal resolution, and may rely on restrictive assumptions about signal or noise statistics. Recently, deep learning has been actively investigated for denoising in biomedical imaging and photoacoustics, as neural networks can learn complex, data-dependent noise characteristics directly from measurements. Convolutional neural network (CNN)-based models have shown considerable success in PAI-related processing tasks. However, many high-performing CNN denoisers become computationally heavy as network depth and receptive field increase, while operations such as pooling are often undesirable because FF-PAT denoising requires preservation of fine wavefront structures. Alternative designs, including non-local networks, trainable nonlinear reaction-diffusion models, pseudo-siamese architectures, PDE-inspired learning frameworks, and autoencoder-based models, offer different trade-offs, but may still be dataset-specific, computationally intensive, or prone to losing fine structural information under severe noise. Recent methods, such as boosting-based networks and hybrid filter–deep models, show promise; nevertheless, there remains a need for a computationally efficient and robust denoising framework that generalizes across noise conditions while preserving wavefront-consistent displacement features critical for FF-PAT reconstruction. In addition, most existing learning-based denoisers for biomedical imaging are designed for intensity images and use fixed feature connectivity, which can be suboptimal for FF-PAT displacement maps where noise statistics vary across space and time. Moreover, many approaches focus on visual quality improvements without demonstrating how denoising affects downstream FF-PAT image reconstruction. This motivates a denoising framework that (i) adapts its internal residual feature integration to varying noise conditions and (ii) explicitly targets preservation of physics-consistent structures associated with propagating photoacoustic wavefronts.

In this work, we propose an Adaptive Residual Attention Network (ARAN) for physics-consistent denoising of FF-PAT displacement maps. Here, the term physics-consistent refers to preservation of the wavefront morphology expected during photoacoustic wave propagation, rather than enforcement of an explicit physical model (e.g., a wave-equation residual or temporal propagation loss) during optimization. The key methodological novelty is a noise-adaptive residual connectivity mechanism: the Adaptive Residual Fusion Module (ARFM) learns input-dependent fusion weights that dynamically regulate how intermediate residual features are integrated, thereby allowing the network to reconfigure its information flow according to the noise characteristics of the input. Unlike fixed skip connections or fixed concatenation, ARFM performs noise-adaptive residual pathway fusion by generating weights that control cross-block residual contributions, enabling dynamic residual connectivity. In addition, we introduce a parameter-free Residual Self-Recalibration (RSR) mechanism that modulates features through joint spatial–channel attention to emphasize wavefront-consistent structures and suppress stochastic fluctuations (i.e., it adds no extra trainable parameters and does not require a separate attention branch). By integrating ARFM and RSR within the hierarchical residual attention mapping (HRAM) stage, ARAN achieves a balance between denoising accuracy and computational efficiency for practical FF-PAT workflows.

Measurement-specific problem formulation: We address denoising of holographically captured FF-PAT displacement maps, where preserving wavefront geometry is essential because errors propagate into the volumetric image reconstructed from the surface maps.

Noise-adaptive residual connectivity: We introduce ARFM, which learns input-dependent fusion of residual pathways, enabling adaptive feature integration under heterogeneous noise conditions beyond fixed residual designs.

Wavefront-preserving attention: We introduce a parameter-free RSR recalibration mechanism that enhances wavefront-consistent structures without increasing model size, yielding a lightweight design suitable for fast inference.

Reconstruction-relevant validation: We demonstrate that ARAN enables volumetric reconstruction quality comparable to that achieved by 50-frame coherent averaging and validate its downstream impact using experimentally acquired data.
The main contributions of this work are as follows:

Overall, this adaptive design enables ARAN to represent displacement features as flexible linear combinations of outputs from preceding residual units. It provides richer and more noise-tolerant representations than static lightweight architectures, and supports faster and clearer FF-PAT image reconstruction.
Proposed methodology
Network structure

The objective of the proposed method, ARAN, is to denoise displacement maps acquired in FF-PAT. The displacement map, denoted as , represents the 2-dimentional map of the surface deformation induced by photoacoustic waves and detected using optical holography. In practice, it contains a combination of stochastic noise (e.g., optical shot/electronic noise and speckle-related fluctuations) and structured disturbances (e.g., phase instability and interference patterns), which degrades displacement-map fidelity and subsequently deteriorates volumetric reconstruction quality. Because FF-PAT image reconstruction is highly sensitive to wavefront distortions, ARAN is designed to suppress noise while preserving wavefront boundaries and displacement discontinuities that reflect photoacoustic wave propagation.
Initial Feature Projection (IFP): projects the measured displacement map into a compact feature space.
Hierarchical Residual Attention Mapping (HRAM): extracts multi-scale residual features while selectively enhancing wavefront-consistent patterns.
Final Refinement Module (FRM): reconstructs the denoised displacement map by fusing shallow and deep features.

Overview of the proposed adaptive residual attention network (ARAN). (a) The overall architecture (top) and the internal structure of an RAU (bottom). IFP projects the input noisy map into a compact feature space, HRAM extracts multi-scale residual features while enhancing wavefront-consistent patterns, and FRM reconstructs the denoised output map. IFP: initial feature projection layer, HRAM: hierarchical residual attention mapping module, RAU: residual attention unit, FRM: final refinement module, RB: residual block, ARFM: adaptive residual fusion module, RSR: residual self-recalibration mechanism. (b) Detailed configurations of RB and ARFM. The RB is composed of cascaded convolutional layers and PReLU activations, whereas the ARFM is composed of two 1 × 1 convolution layers with an intermediate PReLU activation, followed by global average pooling (GAP).
ARAN learns a direct mapping from a single noisy displacement map to its corresponding clean map. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), the network comprises three main stages:
1) Initial Feature Projection (IFP)
Given  (H=W=256), a shallow convolutional operator projects the input into an initial feature tensor:

Here,  is a single convolutional layer that captures local spatial correlations in the displacement field. The resulting feature map  preserves the essential morphology of the photoacoustic waves in the displacement map and serves as a reference for deeper feature extraction in subsequent stages of the network.
2) Hierarchical Residual Attention Mapping (HRAM)

The HRAM stage refines features through a cascade of  residual attention units (RAUs) arranged hierarchically, and each unit has N residual blocks (RBs), as shown in Fig. 1. Each RAU captures higher-order correlations among displacement patterns, including edge-like discontinuities associated with the wavefront shapes of photoacoustic pulses. Given the shallow feature  from the IFP stage, HRAM processes features sequentially as:where (.) denotes the mapping of the k-th RAU, is its input, and  its output. Through this hierarchical refinement, ARAN progressively suppresses noise while preserving physically meaningful photoacoustic-wave patterns relevant to FF-PAT reconstruction. As illustrated in the bottom subfigure of Fig. 1(a), each RAU is composed of RBs and integrates two complementary mechanisms: the ARFM module, which generates input-dependent fusion

Signal flow inside an RAU and the operation of n-th RSR unit. For the k-th RAU, the ARFM takes the input feature  and generates the fusion-coefficient vector . At the n-th RB, the previous feature  is transformed by the RB, while the corresponding subset of ARFM coefficients is used to compute the dynamically fused residual feature  from the preceding features …, These terms are then combined and recalibrated by the sigmoid-based coefficient to produce the output feature , which is passed to the next RB.

coefficients for cross-block residual paths, and a series of N RSR modules, which recalibrates features to emphasize wavefront-consistent responses while suppressing stochastic fluctuations. Each RB consists of cascaded units in which a PReLU activation is applied before a 3 × 3 convolutional layer. The configurations of the RB and ARFM are shown in Fig. 1(b). For the k-th RAU, we define , where  denotes the input feature of that RAU. The subsequent residual features generated within the same RAU are denoted by , ,…  The signal flow inside an RAU, including the operation of RSR, is shown in Fig. 2. At the n-th iteration, the previous feature  is transformed by the RB to generate , while the ARFM coefficients are used to adaptively fuse the preceding features , ,…  into . The sum of these two components is then modulated by a sigmoid-based recalibration coefficient, producing the output feature , which is propagated to the subsequent iteration.
(a) Adaptive Residual Fusion Module (ARFM)

FF-PAT displacement maps often exhibit spatially varying noise characteristics, such as stronger fluctuations in low-signal regions and speckle-like disturbances in relatively homogeneous areas. To handle such variability, ARFM learns fusion coefficients that regulate how information from preceding residual paths is combined. ARFM is evaluated once per RAU using the RAU input feature  and applies two convolutional layers ( and ), a PReLU activation, and global average pooling to produce the complete fusion-weight vector for all residual paths in that RAU:where (.) denotes the ARFM operation. Each element of the vector  controls the strength of a residual path, enabling the network to adaptively adjust its connectivity in response to noise statistics. The output vector  which contains all fusion coefficients required by the N RBs in the k-th RAU, is defined as  Hence, the total number of fusion coefficients generated by ARFM for one RAU is  Each coefficient  controls the contribution of the preceding feature  to the dynamically fused residual feature used by the n-th RB, where . For the n-th RB, the corresponding subset of fusion coefficients is . These coefficients are then used to form the dynamically fused residual feature.

Although ARFM uses weighting coefficients, it is not a conventional attention module. Standard attention blocks such as Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) and the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) primarily reweight channels and/or spatial locations within a single feature tensor. In contrast, ARFM computes cross-block fusion weights that regulate how outputs from multiple preceding residual blocks contribute to the dynamically fused residual feature. This differs from fixed residual addition and fixed concatenation, and enables noise-adaptive residual connectivity for heterogeneous noise in FF-PAT displacement maps.
(b) Residual Self-Recalibration (RSR)

Following adaptive fusion, the n-th RB refines its feature through self-recalibration to emphasize the structures consistent with photoacoustic wave propagation. The output of the n-th RB after RSR is given by:where  denotes the convolutional transform of the n-th RB,  is the dynamically fused residual feature,  is the recalibration coefficient, and dot denotes element-wise multiplication. The recalibration coefficient is computed as:where (⋅) denotes the sigmoid function. RSR is referred to as parameter-free because it introduces no additional trainable parameters beyond those already present in (⋅); the recalibration coefficient α is obtained by applying a sigmoid gate to the residual features, without using a separate attention branch. The dynamic residual feature  is defined as:

Here,  is the input feature of the k-th RAU, while are the outputs of the preceding RBs within the same RAU, and  are the fusion weights defined in Eq. (3). This formulation enables each block to flexibly integrate information from multiple preceding features and to adaptively reconfigure effective residual connectivity by emphasizing or suppressing specific preceding block outputs based on the input noise characteristics. After all N RBs have been processed, the RAU output is obtained by aggregating the RAU input feature and all RB outputs:where concat[⋅] denotes channel-wise concatenation and  denotes a 1 × 1 convolution used to compress the concatenated features into a compact representation. This aggregation preserves both low-level details and high-level photoacoustic features in a compact representation.

RSR attention maps for three representative FF-PAT displacement sample maps. (a-1), (b-1), and (c-1) show the input noisy maps, while (a-2), (b-2), and (c-2) show the corresponding RSR attention maps generated by ARAN, highlighting the spatial–channel recalibration that emphasizes acoustic wavefronts and suppresses background noise.

Visualization of ARFM weight matrices for three representative FF-PAT displacement maps of Fig. 3. For each map, three matrices corresponding to the three RAUs in the network are shown in panels (a-1)–(a-3), (b-1)–(b-3), and (c-1)–(c-3), respectively. These matrices represent the adaptive fusion weights learned by the ARFM for each RAU. The matrix element of brighter color indicates stronger fusion connection.

To further interpret the internal behavior of ARAN, we visualize the learned attention mechanisms. Fig. 3 presents RSR attention maps for three representative samples, illustrating spatial–channel recalibration that emphasizes photoacoustic wavefront regions while suppressing background noise and speckle fluctuations. Fig. 4 shows the corresponding ARFM weight matrices, illustrating how RBs within each RAU adaptively combine features from preceding blocks under varying noise characteristics. Regions with higher intensity values indicate stronger fusion connections, whereas darker regions correspond to weaker or inactive pathways. The evolution of these matrices across RAUs suggests that ARAN progressively reconfigures residual pathways to suppress noise while preserving the continuity of photoacoustic waves. These input-dependent weight patterns confirm that ARFM does not implement a fixed aggregation structure; instead, it learns noise-adaptive residual-path fusion that dynamically reweights the cross-block feature integration.
3) Final Refinement Module (FRM)

To produce the final denoised displacement map, the deep feature  is combined with the shallow feature  through residual learning, ensuring that both high-level contextual information and fine-scale details contribute to the output. The fused representation is then passed to the final refinement module (FRM), which consists of a single convolutional layer that reconstructs the denoised displacement map as:

This design preserves the high spatial frequency components present in the original displacement map, caused by sharp edges of the photoacoustic waves, while effectively suppressing noise through the refined feature extraction performed in earlier stages.
Advantages of ARAN
The proposed ARAN framework offers several advantages over existing denoising methods.

Through the ARFM, the network dynamically adjusts residual connectivity according to input noise statistics, while the RSR mechanism promotes wavefront-consistent feature modulation by amplifying wavefront-aligned structures and attenuating random fluctuations. In particular, ARFM introduces noise-adaptive residual pathway fusion, which is distinct from fixed residual addition/concatenation and improves robustness especially to spatially varying noise. Unlike conventional filtering approaches such as Wiener filtering, total variation minimization, or wavelet thresholding, which rely on fixed priors and often oversmooth high- frequency details, ARAN preserves sharp edges and structural boundaries that are essential for accurate photoacoustic image reconstruction. Furthermore, by avoiding artificial upsampling and performing end-to-end denoising directly on the experimental grid, ARAN distinguishes itself from both classical filters and earlier CNN-based super-resolution methods, ensuring efficient and robust reconstruction of displacement maps.
Implementation details of ARAN.

The implementation details of ARAN are summarized in Table 1. The network begins with an IFP layer, followed by K RAUs, each containing N RBs. Each RB consists of two 3 × 3 convolutional layers with c feature channels and PReLU activations, and its output is formed by combining the current residual transform with the dynamically fused residual feature, followed by RSR. The RSR module applies a sigmoid-based gating coefficient  to recalibrate the feature response and introduces no additional trainable parameters beyond those already present in the residual transform. The ARFM generates fusion weights using two 1 × 1 convolutional layers, with an intermediate channel reduction to 16, followed by a PReLU

activation and global average pooling (GAP). The output width of the ARFM, denoted by , depends on the number of residual blocks N and is given by = [N(N + 1)]/2 which corresponds to the total number of unique fusion coefficients required to model all preceding residual-path contributions within one RAU. In the HRAM stage, the input feature and all residual-block outputs are aggregated through channel-wise concatenation followed by a 1 × 1 convolution. Finally, the FRM applies a 3 × 3 convolution to reconstruct the denoised single-channel displacement map.
Loss function

A dynamic hybrid loss function is employed to train the network, combining the mean squared error (MSE) and the structural similarity index measure (SSIM). This design aims to balance pixel-wise accuracy with structural fidelity in the denoised displacement maps. The loss is formulated as:

penalizes point-wise deviations between the predicted and the ground-truth displacement fields, while  encourages preservation of structural correlations relevant to the continuity of photoacoustic waves. The weighting parameter λ ∈ [0,1] is scheduled to increase gradually during training: early epochs emphasize MSE (λ ≈ 0) to stabilize optimization and capture the overall displacement distribution, whereas later epochs increase λ toward 1 to prioritize structural fidelity and sharper wavefront preservation. This adaptive scheduling strategy allows the network to first achieve accurate pixel-wise reconstruction and subsequently refine its predictions to better preserve structural integrity and edge details that are critical for reliable FF-PAT image reconstruction.
Results
Training and evaluation criteria

Representative displacement maps used for training and evaluation of ARAN under different noise conditions. Displacement map with (a) experimental noise only, (b) experimental and Gaussian noise (SNR = 100 dB), (c) experimental and speckle noise (variance = 0.07), (d) experimental, Gaussian, and speckle noise. (e) Corresponding ground-truth displacement map obtained by coherent averaging of 50 maps acquired under identical conditions. The color bar indicates normalized displacement in arbitrary unit (a.u.). Scale bar: 2 mm.

Training and evaluation were performed using time-resolved surface displacement maps acquired with a custom-built FF-PAT system (Ref.). Each dataset consists of a sequence of displacement maps recorded at multiple excitation–detection time delays. The experimental setup and the acquisition procedure are briefly summarized in Section 3.2. A total of 9000 displacement maps were used in this study. The dataset was split into 80% for training (7200 images) and 20% for testing (1800 images). For each time delay, the ground-truth displacement map  was generated by coherently averaging 50 maps captured under identical experimental conditions. The map captured at a given time delay without averaging, called single-shot, was used as the noisy input . In addition to the experimental noise present in single-shot measurements, synthetic noise was added to assess robustness under more challenging conditions. Specifically, additive Gaussian noise was introduced at SNR levels of 10, 50, and 100 dB, and multiplicative speckle noise was applied with variances in the range 0.05–0.10. A mixed-noise dataset combining Gaussian and speckle components was also constructed. Overall, four datasets were evaluated: (i) one containing only experimental noise, (ii) experimental noise combined with Gaussian noise, (iii) experimental noise combined with speckle noise, and (iv) a mixed-noise dataset containing all noise types. Representative examples of these datasets are shown in Fig. 5. All displacement maps (noisy inputs, ground-truth references, and denoised outputs) were normalized to [0,1] to improve numerical stability and ensure a consistent dynamic range across samples. Specifically, each displacement map I is normalized using dataset-wide min–max scaling, I′= (I−)/(−), where  and  are computed once from the entire dataset and then applied consistently to all images. This operation does not destroy physical amplitude information because it is deterministic and invertible; the denoised outputs can be rescaled back to the original range at any time using the same normalization constants. The proposed network was implemented in Keras and trained using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 and batch size 4. Experiments were conducted on a workstation equipped with an Intel i7 CPU and an NVIDIA RTX 2080Ti GPU.

The denoising performance was quantified using five complementary metrics computed between the denoised output and the ground truth : peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR), root mean squared error (RMSE), edge preservation index (EPI), structural similarity index (SSIM), and contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR). PSNR and RMSE measure overall fidelity. EPI evaluates wavefront-boundary preservation by measuring the similarity between the spatial gradient fields of  and . This is particularly relevant in FF-PAT because photoacoustic wavefronts appear as sharp gradient transitions in displacement maps; therefore, EPI serves as a quantitative indicator of physics-consistent, wavefront-preserving denoising beyond visual inspection. SSIM measures structural consistency; and CNR reflects the detectability of meaningful structures relative to background fluctuations.
These metrics are defined as:
Here, MAX = 1 because all displacement maps were normalized to [0,1] using dataset-wide min–max scaling.where,  and N represent the number of pixels in the image.

Here, ∇ denotes the gradient operator; μ and σ represent mean and standard deviation; is the covariance;  and  are small constants for numerical stability; and , and  denote the mean and standard deviation of the region of interest and background, respectively.
Experiments with real data

The experimental FF-PAT dataset were acquired using a pump–probe scheme in an off-axis optical holographic system as described in Ref.. The specimen under imaging was an optically opaque PDMS phantom prepared by mixing silicone elastomer and curing agent at a 10:1 ratio, with TiO2 particles added to introduce optical scattering. Absorbing structures made of black PET (polyethylene terephthalate) fibers were embedded in the phantom and arranged in a pattern of characters ‘AOL’. Photoacoustic excitation was provided by a 532-nm Nd:YAG laser (pulse width: 8 ns, repetition rate: 20 Hz) delivered via Köhler illumination to achieve approximately uniform irradiation (5 mm beam diameter, 15 mJ/cm² fluence). Surface displacements were captured using a pulsed 905-nm probe laser (pulse width: 20 ns, repetition rate: 40 Hz) with an sCMOS (scientific Complementary Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor) camera. Phase maps  were extracted from the holograms. To suppress drift and isolate the motion induced by photoacoustic waves, both holograms acquired with and without excitation were recorded and their difference was used. The displacement was then calculated as , where  is the probe wavelength (905 nm). By increasing the time delay between the excitation and probe laser pulses in 50-ns steps, time-resolved displacement maps showing photoacoustic wave propagation were obtained. The resulting displacement maps were used for training and evaluation as described in Section 3.1.

Evaluation of the proposed ARAN on two representative FF-PAT displacement maps. (a-1), (a-2): Input displacement maps with experimental noise. (b-1), (b-2): Ground-truth displacement maps obtained by coherent averaging of 50 frames. (c-1), (c-2): Denoised displacement maps produced by the proposed ARAN model. (d-1), (d-2): Corresponding EPI maps highlighting the regions of preserved wavefront structures. The color bar indicates normalized displacement (a.u.). Scale bar: 2 mm.

We first evaluated ARAN on displacement maps dominated by real measurement noise. Two representative displacement maps acquired from the same specimen but at different time delays are shown in Figs. 6(a-1)–6(a-2). Due to low SNR, the raw inputs exhibit strong random fluctuations that distort wavefront boundaries. The corresponding ground-truth maps obtained via 50-frame coherent averaging (Figs. 6(b-1)–6(b-2)) reveal clear and continuous photoacoustic wavefront structures. ARAN outputs (Figs. 6(c-1)–6(c-2)) effectively suppress background noise while preserving fine spatial details and wavefront continuity. The EPI maps in Figs. 6(d-1)–6(d-2) further support this observation, with high-EPI regions aligning with wavefront transitions in the averaged reference. Since the wavefronts are characterized by localized high-gradient transitions, this spatial correspondence indicates that ARAN preserves wavefront boundaries without introducing excessive smoothing.
Quantitative evaluation of denoising performance of the proposed ARAN on the two displacement map samples of Fig. 6.

ARAN restores coherent wavefront structures and suppresses random fluctuations (Figs. 7(c-1)–7(c-2)), while maintaining sharp wavefront boundaries, as reflected in the EPI maps (Figs. 7(d-1)–7(d-2)). Quantitatively (Table 3), for the mild noise case (100 dB), ARAN achieves PSNR = 28.74 dB, RMSE = 0.044, EPI = 0.72, SSIM = 0.842, and CNR = 17.96 dB, indicating accurate reconstruction with minimal distortion. Even under the more challenging (50 dB) condition, ARAN maintains strong performance with PSNR = 27.83 dB, RMSE = 0.052, EPI= 0.68, SSIM = 0.821, and CNR = 16.74 dB. These results demonstrate that ARAN remains robust under increased Gaussian noise while preserving edge definition and structural fidelity.

Quantitative results reported in Table 2 further validate these observations. For the two maps, ARAN achieves PSNR = 30.56 dB and 32.84 dB with RMSE = 0.039 and 0.028, respectively. The EPI values (0.76 and 0.73) indicate strong preservation of wavefront edges. High SSIM values demonstrate close structural agreement with the averaged reference, while improved CNR confirms enhanced contrast between signal-dominant regions and background noise. Overall, ARAN yields displacement maps comparable to the 50-frame averaged references while requiring only a single-shot input.

To assess stability under harsher conditions, we further corrupted the experimentally acquired displacement maps with synthetic Gaussian and speckle noises. Gaussian noise simulates reduced-SNR acquisition conditions (e.g., lower excitation strength or increased environmental perturbations), while speckle noise captures multiplicative fluctuations commonly encountered in holographic measurements.

The most challenging evaluation combines experimental noise with both Gaussian and speckle components (Fig. 9). A mild mixed-noise condition (Gaussian 100 dB, speckle variance 0.06) and a severe condition (Gaussian 10 dB, speckle variance 0.08) are shown. Despite significant wavefront distortion in the inputs, ARAN preserves continuous wavefront geometry and retains sharp transitions in the EPI maps. The very low-intensity regions in the displayed maps correspond to the lower end of the displacement range after dataset-wide normalization and therefore should not be interpreted directly as “negative-valued patches” in the displayed range (e.g., Figs. 9(c-2)). The faint rectangular background structures that are occasionally visible are not wavefront-consistent and are attributed to residual system/

A comprehensive comparison between ARAN and existing deep learning-based denoising methods was performed across multiple noise conditions, as illustrated in Fig. 10. The first row (a-1)–(a-4) shows input displacement maps under experimental noise only, experimental and Gaussian noise (50 dB), experimental and speckle noise (variance 0.06), and mixed noise (Gaussian 50 dB and speckle variance 0.065). The corresponding ground-truth maps obtained via coherent averaging are shown in (b-1)–(b-4). Denoised outputs from ARAN are presented in (c-1)–(c-4), followed by results from U-Net and Noise2Noise in (d-1)–(d-4) and (e-1)–(e-4), respectively. Visual comparison reveals that ARAN consistently produces the cleanest and most structurally accurate displacement maps across all noise scenarios, effectively removing high-frequency fluctuations and speckle patterns while preserving sharp and continuous wavefronts. In contrast, U-Net and Noise2Noise exhibit noticeable smoothing

passages.text: are reported in Table 7. This non-parametric paired test was chosen because image-quality metrics may deviate from normality across large datasets. As shown in Table 7, ARAN achieves statistically significant improvements over both baselines across PSNR, RMSE, EPI, SSIM, and CNR under all evaluated noise conditions. The corresponding p-values confirm that the observed gains are consistent across the test population.

Table 6 summarizes the quantitative comparison. ARAN achieves superior performance across all evaluation metrics, including PSNR, SSIM, EPI, and CNR, while maintaining the lowest RMSE. These improvements confirm that the adaptive residual attention mechanisms embedded in ARAN enable more effective feature restoration and enhanced structural fidelity compared with conventional encoder–decoder and noise-to-noise learning frameworks.

Mean ± standard deviation of all evaluation metrics are reported for ARAN, U-Net, and Noise2Noise (N2N) under all evaluated noise conditions. P-values were computed using a two-tailed paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on per-image metric values, comparing ARAN against U-Net and ARAN against N2N.

For generalized dataset-level evaluation, all quantitative metrics were computed for each test image (N = 1800), and results are summarized as mean ± standard deviation in Table 7. To assess whether ARAN’s improvements over U-Net and Noise2Noise (N2N) are statistically significant across the full test set, we performed a two-tailed paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test on per-image metric values for each noise condition and each metric; the resulting p-values

Computational efficiency was evaluated by measuring average denoising times over 100 displacement maps of size 256 × 256 pixels. Coherent averaging, which requires acquisition and averaging of 50 frames, exhibited the longest processing time of approximately 2.5 s per image. In contrast, ARAN achieved an average processing time of 0.53 s per image, demonstrating its suitability for near real-time and high-throughput FF-PAT imaging. U-Net and Noise2Noise required 0.78 s and 0.91 s, respectively, due to their larger encoder–decoder architectures and higher computational overhead. Despite incorporating dual attention modules, ARAN maintained the fastest inference among the learning-based approaches, highlighting its optimized design for efficient and accurate displacement map denoising.
Ablation study

To assess the individual contributions of the attention components within ARAN, an ablation study was conducted using two modified network variants trained under identical experimental conditions, including the same data splits, optimizer, learning rate, and loss schedule. The evaluated variants include: (A1) ARAN without the ARFM, and (A2) ARAN without the RSR mechanism. The full model and both ablated versions were tested on four representative displacement maps containing: (a) experimental noise only, (b) experimental and Gaussian noise (SNR = 50 dB), (c) experimental and speckle noise (variance = 0.06), and (d) mixed noise

Ablation study illustrating the contribution of ARFM and RSR in ARAN. (a-1)–(a-4) Input noisy displacement maps under experimental, Gaussian (50 dB), speckle (variance = 0.06), and mixed (Gaussian 50 dB + speckle 0.065) noises. (b-1)–(b-4) Ground-truth maps. (c-1)–(c-4) Denoised results of full ARAN. (d-1)–(d-4) Outputs of w/o ARFM (fixed residual paths). (e-1)–(e-4) Outputs of w/o RSR (no self-recalibration). The color bar indicates normalized displacement (a.u.). Scale bar: 2 mm.

In Fig. 11, the first row (a-1)–(a-4) shows the noisy input displacement maps under the four noise conditions, while corresponding ground-truth maps obtained via coherent averaging are displayed in the second row (b-1)–(b-4). The denoised outputs produced by the complete ARAN model are presented in (c-1)–(c-4), consistently yielding smooth and clean displacement fields with well-preserved acoustic wavefronts and sharp, localized edges. In contrast, the variant A1 (without ARFM), shown in (d-1)–(d-4), exhibits incomplete noise suppression and noticeably blurred boundaries, particularly in regions containing complex or overlapping fields with well-preserved acoustic wavefronts and sharp, localized edges. In contrast, the variant A1 (without ARFM), shown in (d-1)–(d-4), exhibits incomplete noise suppression and noticeably blurred boundaries, particularly in regions containing complex or overlapping wavefronts. This degradation arises because fixed residual connectivity lacks the flexibility to adapt to spatially varying noise, leading to weaker feature fusion and reduced contextual representation. Removing ARFM converts ARAN to a fixed-connectivity residual design, which reduces the model’s ability to adapt feature integration to heterogeneous noise and leads to degradation in wavefront-boundary preservation. The variant A2 (without RSR), shown in (e-1)–(e-4), retains the overall wavefront structure but displays reduced local contrast and faded edges with background noise. This behavior confirms that the self-recalibration mechanism in RSR plays a critical role in enhancing wavefront-aligned displacement features and suppressing residual noise through spatial–channel modulation.
Quantitative ablation results comparing Full ARAN with A1 (w/o ARFM) and A2 (w/o RSR) under the four noise conditions (corresponding to Fig. 11).

passages.text: Quantitative results reported in Table 8 further support these visual observations. The complete ARAN model consistently achieves the highest PSNR and SSIM values, indicating superior reconstruction fidelity and structural consistency, along with the lowest RMSE across all noise conditions. The EPI scores of the full network (approximately 0.69–0.76) are notably higher than those of the ablated variants (approximately 0.61–0.71), highlighting the importance of ARFM in preserving edge continuity through adaptive residual fusion. Similarly, ARAN attains consistently higher CNR values (approximately 17–19 dB) compared with the ablated models (approximately 13–16 dB), confirming improved contrast recovery between acoustic wavefronts and background regions.

Removing ARFM results in an average PSNR reduction of approximately 2.0–2.3 dB and moderate edge softening, while eliminating RSR causes a more pronounced decrease in SSIM (approximately 0.03–0.05) and CNR (approximately 2.5 dB), reflecting weaker spatial–channel recalibration and diminished local contrast. Overall, these results demonstrate that ARFM and RSR provide complementary benefits: ARFM enables adaptive integration of residual features under heterogeneous noise conditions, while RSR reinforces physically consistent wavefront structures. Their combined effect allows ARAN to achieve an optimal balance among accuracy, sharpness, and contrast, ensuring robust denoising performance across diverse FF-PAT noise scenarios.
Discussion and conclusion

The proposed Adaptive Residual Attention Network (ARAN) improves single-shot denoising of FF-PAT surface displacement maps across experimental measurements and augmented noise conditions while preserving wavefront gradients that are essential for accurate reconstruction. In particular, ARAN consistently retains the continuity and sharpness of high- gradient wavefront features that are often attenuated by baseline CNN denoisers under severe noise, leading to displacement maps that better support downstream tomographic reconstruction.

The performance gain can be attributed to the complementary roles of ARFM and RSR. ARFM provides noise-adaptive residual fusion by learning input-dependent fusion weights that regulate how intermediate residual features are combined. This adaptive connectivity is especially beneficial for FF-PAT measurements, where noise statistics vary not only spatially but also temporally, enabling localized suppression of distortions (e.g., speckle-like fluctuations and structured boundary artifacts) without oversmoothing wavefront transitions. RSR further refines the fused representation through spatial–channel recalibration, selectively emphasizing coherent wavefront-aligned patterns while attenuating residual stochastic
variations. Together, these mechanisms improve structural consistency and contrast while maintaining computational efficiency.

Quantitatively, ARAN achieves improved PSNR, SSIM, and CNR, together with reduced RMSE, compared with the evaluated baselines, with consistently high EPI indicating strong preservation of wavefront gradients even under challenging noise conditions. Notably, ARAN approaches the quality of 50-frame coherent averaging with a single-shot measurement, substantially reducing acquisition burden and supporting faster process. Compared with U-Net and Noise2Noise, which tend to smooth high-gradient regions and partially attenuate fine wavefront structure, ARAN more reliably preserves the sharp transitions and coherent propagation patterns of photoacoustic waves across all tested scenarios.

Photoacoustic image reconstruction results. (a-1) and (a-2) show the 2D and 3D images reconstructed from noisy displacement maps, respectively. (b-1) and (b-2) present the corresponding reconstructions from the ARAN-denoised displacement maps. Scale bar: 2 mm.

Beyond displacement-map quality, the ultimate goal of FF-PAT is accurate 3D reconstruction of the optical absorbers distributed within a host medium. To demonstrate the impact of denoising on reconstruction quality, in Fig. 12, we present the 2D and 3D reconstructed images obtained from the single-shot maps by using the single-sheet backpropagation (SSB) algorithm described in Ref.. The images reconstructed from noisy displacement single-shot maps (Figs. 12(a-1)–12(a-2)) exhibit pronounced artifacts and reduced structural clarity, whereas the ones reconstructed from the ARAN-denoised displacement maps (Figs. 12(b-1)–12(b-2)) show highly improved contrast with reduced noise. Reconstruction in this study was performed using normalized displacement values. Because the applied dataset-wide normalization is a global linear rescaling, it preserves the relative spatial and temporal structure of the displacement field; therefore, the reconstruction differs only by an overall amplitude scaling factor, while the structural reconstruction results remain unchanged. It is noted that the larger field of view in Fig. 12 was obtained by stitching four adjacent reconstructed regions, whereas the displacement maps shown elsewhere in this article were acquired without stitching.

Despite these improvements, ARAN is trained on data acquired using a specific FF-PAT configuration (Ref.), and performance may degrade under substantial domain shifts (e.g., different optical surfaces, probe stability, or different artifact patterns). Consistent with this, faint rectangular low-intensity background patches occasionally observed in displacement maps are attributed to residual system/processing artifacts rather than physical propagation of photoacoustic waves; their impact on reconstruction is limited due to their low amplitude and the reconstruction’s reliance on wavefront-consistent gradients. Future work will therefore focus on improving generalization across experimental conditions (e.g., multi-domain training and robustness to structured artifacts) and exploring direct learning-based volumetric image reconstruction from the sequences of surface displacement maps. Incorporating the explicit physics-informed constraints is also a promising direction to further enhance interpretability and reliability in complex optical–acoustic environments.

In conclusion, ARAN provides a noise-adaptive and efficient denoising framework for FF-PAT displacement maps. By combining dynamic residual fusion with lightweight self-recalibration, ARAN suppresses stochastic and multiplicative noise while preserving physics-consistent features, specifically, the characteristics of propagating photoacoustic wavefronts that are critical for volumetric image reconstruction, enabling near–coherent-averaging performance from a single-shot measurement.
